title: Rwanda's clinical trial capacity relative to peer low-income countries
primary_estimand: Rwanda Ratio (Rwanda trials per million / peer-country trials per million) for 10 income- and size-matched comparators
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration of ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: Can a low-income African country achieve clinical trial density comparable to higher-income regional peers through deliberate capacity-building partnerships? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for interventional trials in Rwanda, ten peer countries matched by population or income, and benchmarks including South Africa, Uganda, and the United States. Rwanda registered 121 trials for 14 million people, yielding 8.6 per million, exceeding most peers by wide margins, with the Rwanda Ratio ranging from two-fold to above twenty-fold and neighbouring Burundi at just 1.0 per million despite similar population and shared history. Rwanda also showed broader condition coverage than peers that remained concentrated in infectious-disease portfolios. Growth since 2015 outpaced most comparators, suggesting sustained institutional development rather than a transient single-project effect. Candidate explanations include centralized ethics review, durable partnerships such as the Einstein-Rwanda programme, and governmental commitment to research infrastructure. The analysis is limited to one registry and cannot attribute observed differences to any single policy mechanism.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

references.citation: Hedt-Gauthier BL, et al. Building research capacity through a hospital-based research partnership: the experience of Einstein and Rwanda. BMC Glob Public Health. 2025;3:12.

checks.name: primary estimate in sentences 3-4
checks.detail: Sentences 3 and 4 describe the Rwanda Ratio and peer comparison pattern.
